title: The Quiet Abdication
synopsis: I have been watching the American alliance from Adelaide with the particular attention of someone whose national security depends on it. The attent...
preview: I have been watching the American alliance from Adelaide with the particular attention of someone whose national security depends on it. The attent...
content:
I have been watching the American alliance from Adelaide with the particular attention of someone whose national security depends on it. The attention is uncomfortable, the attention of a tenant who has noticed cracks in the ceiling and is trying to determine whether they are cosmetic or structural.

Six months into the Trump presidency, I think they are structural.

I want to be careful here because the discourse around Trump is so polarised that any observation about American strategic direction gets immediately sorted into partisan categories. Whether Trump is a good or bad president matters less to me than what his election, and the doctrine emerging from it, reveals about the durability of the security architecture that Australia has depended on for seventy years.

America First is not a slogan. It is a strategic reorientation. The idea that American alliances should be transactional, that the United States should calculate the cost and benefit of each commitment and withdraw from those that do not pay, is not a Trumpian invention. It has deep roots in American political culture. But it has never before been the explicit framework of a sitting administration.

NATO is wobbling. Not collapsing. Wobbling. The language of unconditional commitment has been replaced by the language of burden-sharing. The Article 5 guarantee, an attack on one is an attack on all, has been publicly questioned by the American president. Not repudiated, but questioned, which in alliance politics amounts to something similar. An alliance whose guarantor expresses doubts about whether the guarantee is worth honouring has already changed, regardless of whether the guarantee is ever formally withdrawn.

From Australia, this matters more than most Australians appreciate. Our entire strategic posture is built on the assumption that the United States will, when it matters, show up. ANZUS is the foundation. Pine Gap, the joint facilities, the interoperability of our military forces with American forces, all of it rests on the premise that the alliance is durable. That American power projection into the Indo-Pacific is permanent. That we can trade freely with Asia under the protective umbrella of American naval dominance, and that this arrangement will continue indefinitely.

We have no Plan B.

I find this extraordinary. A country of twenty-five million people, occupying an entire continent, surrounded by water, dependent on maritime trade for virtually everything, with no independent capacity to secure its own sea lanes, and no serious contingency planning for a world in which the American guarantee weakens or disappears.

I have visited countries that take their own security seriously. Singapore, with a population smaller than Sydney, maintains a military capability that is, relative to its size, one of the most formidable in the world. Conscription is universal. Defence spending is substantial. The strategic culture is one of self-reliance tempered by alliance, not dependence disguised as alliance.

Israel, surrounded by states that have at various points sought its destruction, has built an indigenous defence industry that produces some of the most advanced military technology on earth. When Israel enters an alliance, it does so as a partner with capability, not as a dependent seeking protection.

Switzerland, which has not fought a war in five hundred years, maintains universal military service and an arms industry that exports globally. The neutrality is active, not passive. It is backed by the capacity for self-defence.

Australia's approach is different. We have outsourced our security to the United States with a thoroughness that would be impressive if it were not so reckless. We do not manufacture our own fighter aircraft. We do not build our own submarines in any meaningful sense, though we talk about it endlessly. Our intelligence capabilities are deeply integrated with American systems in ways that make independent operation difficult. Our strategic planning assumes American presence in the region as a given, not as a variable.

This has worked beautifully for seventy years. The American umbrella held. The trade routes stayed open. The region remained, by historical standards, remarkably stable. We grew wealthy beneath the umbrella and invested the wealth in housing rather than capability.

The question I keep returning to is what happens when the umbrella frays. Not collapses, frays. The distinction matters. A sudden American withdrawal from the region would be a crisis that would command attention. A gradual reduction in commitment, a slow shift of priorities, a progressive narrowing of the circumstances under which the guarantee applies, this is harder to see and harder to respond to.

I think this is what we are watching. Not an abdication but a qualification. America First means America's interests first, and there is no guarantee that America's interests in the Indo-Pacific will always align with ours. The alliance has always contained an asymmetry, we need it more than they do, but the asymmetry was masked by the cold war, by shared values, by the habit of cooperation. Remove the cold war framework, introduce a president who thinks explicitly in transactional terms, and the asymmetry becomes visible.

What should Australia do? The honest answer is that I do not know. The options are limited by decades of decisions that narrowed them.

We could invest seriously in independent defence capability. This would mean spending substantially more than we currently do, over a sustained period, on capabilities that we currently obtain through the alliance. Indigenous manufacturing of weapons systems. Expanded naval capacity. Genuine strategic autonomy, not as a replacement for the alliance but as an insurance policy against its degradation.

We could pursue strategic diversification. Deeper security relationships with Japan, with India, with Indonesia, with the European powers that retain interests in the region. Not as a replacement for the American alliance but as a hedge against over-dependence on a single partner.

We could invest in the forms of national resilience that make a country harder to coerce. Domestic manufacturing capability. Energy independence. Food security. The ability to function, even at reduced capacity, if external support is interrupted.

None of these options are being seriously pursued. The national conversation about defence remains stuck in a comfortable loop: reaffirm the alliance, announce a procurement project, defer the difficult questions to the next electoral cycle.

Meanwhile, in Washington, the language changes. The commitments become conditional. The calculations become explicit. And in Canberra, nobody seems to have noticed, or if they have noticed, nobody seems to know what to do about it.

There is a quality of attention that I think this moment demands. Not panic. Panic is useless. Not denial, which is where we currently reside. Something more like the focused assessment of a patient who has received a diagnosis that may or may not be serious. You do not ignore it. You do not catastrophise. You examine the evidence, consider the possibilities, and begin preparing for the outcomes you would prefer not to face.

The American alliance may endure for decades more. It may strengthen under future administrations. The wobble may prove temporary, the cracks cosmetic.

But a country that depends on an alliance for its survival and does not prepare for the possibility that the alliance may weaken is a country that has confused hope with strategy. And hope, as they say in the military, is not a plan.

The abdication is quiet. It is happening in press conferences and policy documents and the careful language of diplomatic communiqués. It is not dramatic enough to make the evening news in Australia. But it is happening, and the people whose job it is to notice, in Canberra, in the defence establishment, in the foreign policy community, should be paying closer attention than I believe they are.

From Adelaide, I watch. It is all I can do from here. But I watch with the growing conviction that the world my generation inherited is not the world that will persist, and that the sooner we acknowledge this, the better our chances of navigating what comes next.
